text:
DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

--- FINAL REPORT ---

PATIENT: Marlena Okonkwo
MRN: [REDACTED]
SEX: Female
AGE: 39 years
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Oncology attending on record]
ORDERING SERVICE: Medical Oncology
PATHOLOGIST: [Attending Pathologist, MD]

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SPECIMEN IDENTIFICATION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A:
  Designation: Right iliac wing, CT-guided core needle biopsy
  Clinical indication: Lytic osseous lesion, right iliac wing; evaluation for metastatic disease in patient with history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma (triple-negative), treated with neoadjuvant Keytruda/chemotherapy and lumpectomy, now presenting with new bone pain and CT/bone scan findings suspicious for distant recurrence.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CLINICAL HISTORY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The patient is a 39-year-old woman with a prior history of left breast invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative (triple-negative), initially Stage IIB (pT2 pN1a), diagnosed approximately 18 months prior. She was treated with neoadjuvant pembro plus dose-dense Adriamycin/cyclo followed by weekly Taxol/Keytruda, then underwent left partial mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy and axillary lymph node dissection (ypT1c ypN0(i+), RCB-II). Adjuvant therapy included whole breast and regional nodal radiation, cape x6 cycles, and completion Keytruda for a total of 17 cycles. She now presents with new right-sided rib pain and was found to have multiple new lytic osseous lesions and hepatic lesions on cross-sectional imaging and bone scintigraphy. CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right iliac wing lytic lesion was performed for pathologic confirmation.

Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant known (p.E1250*).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
TYPE OF PROCEDURE
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CT-guided core needle biopsy, right iliac wing (image-guided, interventional radiology). Multiple core passes performed under CT fluoroscopic guidance using a coaxial 11-gauge bone biopsy needle system. Four cores submitted in formalin.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS PATHOLOGY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A: Received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and "right iliac wing biopsy." The specimen consists of four cylindrical core biopsy fragments of gray-white to tan-gray tissue with admixed hemorrhage and focal gritty calcified bone trabeculae. Aggregate core length: approximately 1.5 cm total; individual core lengths range from 0.2 to 0.5 cm. Cores are submitted entirely in two cassettes (A1 and A2), with representative cores decalcified prior to sectioning.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Sections demonstrate fragments of bone and marrow space extensively infiltrated by sheets and irregular nests of neoplastic epithelial cells. The neoplastic cells are large with markedly enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei, prominent macronucleoli, and scant to moderate amounts of pale to eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios are high. Mitotic figures are readily identified (estimated >10/10 high-power fields), including atypical forms. Tumor cells are arranged predominantly in solid sheets and irregular trabeculae with focal central comedonecrosis. No glandular/tubular differentiation is identified.

The background bone marrow shows replacement of normal hematopoietic elements by tumor, with associated desmoplastic stroma and scattered reactive osteoclast-like giant cells at the periphery of bony trabeculae. Osteolysis is evident with thinning and focal disruption of residual trabeculae. There is no geographic bone necrosis to suggest prior treatment effect in this specimen.

No lymphovascular invasion is definitively identified within the biopsy cores. No perineural invasion seen.

The histomorphology is that of a poorly differentiated carcinoma with high nuclear grade (Grade 3), consistent with the patient's known triple-negative breast primary.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES -- IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

IHC performed on formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) decalcified bone biopsy material (Block A1). Results interpreted with appropriate positive and negative controls.

  Marker               Clone/Source         Result
  -------              ------               ------
  CK7                  OV-TL-12/30          Positive (diffuse, strong, 3+)
  CK20                 Ks20.8               Negative
  GATA3                L50-823              Positive (moderate to strong, nuclear, ~80% of tumor cells)
  Mammaglobin          31A5                 Focally positive (~20% of tumor cells, weak to moderate)
  GCDFP-15             23A3                 Negative
  TTF-1                8G7G3/1              Negative
  CDX2                 AMT28                Negative
  PAX8                 MRQ50                Negative
  ER (SP1)             SP1/Ventana          Negative (0% nuclear staining; internal positive control: present)
  PR (1E2)             1E2/Ventana          Negative (0% nuclear staining)
  HER2 (4B5)           4B5/Ventana          1+ (incomplete, faint membrane staining; non-amplified; negative per ASCO/CAP 2018 criteria)
  Ki-67                MIB-1                Approximately 72% (hot-spot estimation)
  p53                  DO-7                 Overexpression pattern (strong, diffuse nuclear positivity, consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation)
  E-cadherin           36/E-Cadh            Positive (intact membranous staining, consistent with ductal-type carcinoma)

PD-L1 (22C3 assay, Dako Omnis platform):
  Combined Positive Score (CPS): 8
  Interpretation: PD-L1 CPS 8 -- below the established threshold of CPS >=10 for first-line Keytruda eligibility in the metastatic TNBC setting. Tumor proportion score (TPS): approximately 5%. Immune cell staining: present but limited.
  Note: The PD-L1 CPS on this metastatic specimen (8) is lower than that recorded on the primary diagnostic biopsy (CPS 15). This discordance is noted for clinical correlation and may reflect tumor heterogeneity or immune editing during treatment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A -- Right iliac wing, CT-guided core needle biopsy:

  METASTATIC CARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH BREAST PRIMARY
  -- Poorly differentiated (Grade 3) carcinoma with solid architecture and comedonecrosis
  -- Immunophenotype: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 negative (IHC 1+), CK7 positive, GATA3 positive, mammaglobin focally positive, TTF-1 negative, CK20 negative -- profile consistent with and supportive of metastatic triple-negative breast carcinoma
  -- Ki-67: approximately 72%
  -- PD-L1 CPS (22C3): 8
  -- See immunohistochemistry detail above

Comment:
The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings are consistent with metastatic carcinoma of breast origin in the clinical context of this patient's documented history of high-grade, triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast. The immunoprofile (CK7+, GATA3+, mammaglobin focally+, CK20-, TTF-1-, PAX8-, CDX2-) strongly supports a breast primary and effectively excludes lung, gynecologic, gastrointestinal, and urothelial primaries. The tumor retains the triple-negative receptor profile seen at initial diagnosis (ER-/PR-/HER2- by IHC), with a Ki-67 of ~72% (reduced from 85% at diagnosis, though this comparison is limited by differences in specimen type and processing). The PD-L1 CPS of 8 on this specimen is noted to be lower than the CPS of 15 documented at initial core needle biopsy, a finding that has been reported in the context of prior anti-PD-1 therapy and warrants clinical correlation.

Electron microscopy: Not performed.
Flow cytometry: Not performed.
Molecular/NGS studies: Separately ordered; results to be reported under addendum when available.

Case reviewed with slide sign-out by [Attending Pathologist, MD, Surgical Pathology].
